summary: McKenzie argues that open source software is a powerful strategic asset for proprietary software developers, not a threat. He demonstrates how OSS dramatically reduces development time by letting developers leverage thousands of man-months of existing work while focusing on their unique value-add. He outlines multiple business benefits: lower development costs, SEO advantages from community engagement, and a 'quality cliff' that acts as a barrier to entry against competitors not using OSS. The post reframes the typical proprietary-vs-open-source debate as a false dichotomy, showing how commercial developers can strategically contribute to and consume OSS simultaneously.
key_points: OSS lets solo developers leverage thousands of man-months of existing work while adding only months of their own glue and secret sauce
Rather than open-sourcing your core product, contribute boring infrastructure pieces tangentially connected to your business for social and SEO benefits
OSS creates a 'quality cliff' barrier to entry: competitors not using it must build from scratch what you integrated in minutes
The skill of integrating diverse OSS projects into a coherent product is itself a competitive advantage that's harder than straight application development
OSS developers are high-value linkerati who provide SEO benefits and community engagement even if they never buy your product
money_quotes: I honestly think that it is impossible, flat out impossible, for any one person to be expert at all the technologies which are implicated by a trivial web application running on a modern web stack.

I'm probably literally using several thousand man months worth of software, adding two man months worth of glue and secret sauce, and then if all goes according to plan making a bit of money off of it.
You can't just whistle your fingers and snap together 15 diverse and unconnected OSS projects into a usable application — the design and integration of complex systems is still a skill.

Every time you spend a small quantum of effort to integrate a new feature that you didn't have to write, your user-perceived quality gaps up. Someone running along on your old curve, trying to keep up, runs straight into the quality cliff-face.
Now Bob isn't just running the race against you — he's running against the team at Google doing the Charts development and you're already done and busy working on your next feature.
We live in an era where, for better or worse, GoogleBot is a judge of character and strongly prefers people who share.
OSS developers are typically not poor aesthetics who need to beg for their next cup of coffee.
key_insight: Open source software is not a threat to proprietary developers but a force multiplier — the real competitive advantage lies in the skill of integrating diverse OSS projects into a coherent product that competitors cannot easily replicate.
